title: Original Certificate Retention
summary: Colleges cannot legally hold original student marksheets or certificates against pending fees or to prevent withdrawal.
description: Under UGC and AICTE regulations, institutions are prohibited from retaining original certificates. They are required to return them once the verification is complete. Retention of documents for non-payment of fees is a violation of these guidelines.

title: Anti-Ragging Protection
summary: Strict UGC guidelines penalize mental and physical harassment (ragging) in educational institutions.
description: Ragging is a criminal offense in India. The UGC (Promotion of Equity in Higher Educational Institutions) Regulations and the Supreme Court's mandate make it mandatory for every institution to have an anti-ragging squad and committee. Students can file an FIR if the institution fails to act.

title: Fee Refund on Withdrawal
summary: UGC rules mandate a partial or full refund of fees if a student withdraws before the session starts.
description: According to the UGC Fee Refund Policy, institutions must refund 100% of the fee (minus a processing charge) if a student withdraws 15 days or more before the formally notified last date of admission. This protects students from losing large sums when switching institutions.

title: Internal Assessment Transparency
summary: Students have a right to view their evaluated internal scripts and understand the marking criteria.
description: Most University statutes grant students the right to see their internal assessment marks and scripts before they are submitted to the University. This ensures fairness and allows for correction of technical errors or biased marking.

title: Right to Form Unions
summary: The right to form associations and unions is a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(c) of the Constitution.
description: While institutions can place 'reasonable restrictions' for order, they cannot blanketly ban student associations or unions. This right allows students to collectively represent their grievances to the management.

title: Privacy in Hostels
summary: Hostel residents are entitled to a reasonable expectation of privacy and protection against arbitrary searches.
description: While hostel wardens have the right to inspect premises for illegal activities, arbitrary and frequent searches at odd hours without valid reasons can be challenged as a violation of the right to privacy under Article 21.

title: Safe Campus & Transport
summary: Institutions are legally bound to provide a safe environment and secure transport if they provide it.
description: This includes proper lighting, security personnel, and mandatory character verification for drivers of college buses under the Saksham Guidelines for women's safety on campuses.

title: Mental Health Support
summary: Universities must provide counseling and mental health services to students.
description: The Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and UGC guidelines require universities to have active counseling centers to address student stress, anxiety, and other mental health challenges, ensuring a holistic learning environment.

title: Protection Against Sudden Eviction
summary: Landlords cannot forcefully evict you without a legal order or proper notice period.
description: Under the Rent Control Acts of various states, a tenant can only be evicted on specific grounds like non-payment of rent, misuse of property, or personal necessity of the landlord, and only after a court decree is obtained.

title: Security Deposit Recovery
summary: The landlord must return the security deposit within one month of the tenant vacating.
description: The Model Tenancy Code (and state-specific acts) requires the landlord to return the security deposit after deducting legitimate arrears or repair costs for damage beyond normal wear and tear.

title: Essential Services Maintenance
summary: Landlords cannot cut off water, electricity, or other essential services to force a tenant out.
description: Interrupting essential services is a criminal act under most state Rent Control Acts. Tenants can approach the Rent Controller or a civil court for immediate restoration of these services.

title: Maintenance and Repairs
summary: Structural repairs are the landlord's responsibility unless stated otherwise in the agreement.
description: While day-to-day minor repairs are handled by the tenant, major structural issues (leakages, wiring failures, wall cracks) are statutory responsibilities of the landlord to ensure the property is 'fit for habitation'.

title: Right to FIR Copy (Free)
summary: Every person who files an FIR is entitled to a copy of it free of cost from the police.
description: Under Section 154(2) of the CrPC (and corresponding BNSS sections), the police must provide a copy of the FIR immediately and without any charge to the informant.

title: Right to Information (RTI)
summary: Citizens can request information from public authorities to ensure transparency.
description: The RTI Act, 2005 allows any citizen to seek information about government operations, funding, and decisions, with a mandatory 30-day response window for the Public Information Officer.

title: Free Legal Aid
summary: Article 39A ensures free legal representation for marginalized groups.
description: The Legal Services Authorities Act provides for free legal aid to women, children, members of SC/ST, and those earning below a certain income threshold to ensure 'justice for all'.

title: Right to be Produced before Magistrate
summary: Anyone arrested must be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours of arrest.
description: This is a fundamental right under Article 22(2) of the Constitution and Section 57 of the CrPC. The 24-hour period excludes the time taken for travel from the place of arrest to the court.

title: Right against Night-time Arrest (Women)
summary: A woman cannot be arrested after sunset and before sunrise except in extraordinary circumstances.
description: Section 46(4) of the CrPC mandates that if such an arrest is necessary, a woman police officer must obtain prior permission from a Judicial Magistrate of the first class.

title: Right to Safe Environment
summary: The right to live in a healthy environment is part of the Right to Life under Article 21.
description: The Supreme Court has expanded Article 21 to include the right to clean air and water. Citizens can file Public Interest Litigations (PIL) against pollution or ecological damage.

title: Consumer Right to Choose
summary: Consumers should be assured, wherever possible, access to a variety of goods and services at competitive prices.
description: Under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, this right protects against restrictive trade practices and monopolies that limit consumer choice.

title: Right to 100% Refund (Defective Goods)
summary: Consumers have the right to seek replacement or full refund for defective products.
description: If a product has a 'manufacturing defect' or the service is 'deficient', the consumer can approach the District Consumer Commission for a full refund along with compensation for harassment.
